content.full_text:
State of California 
f,,'lemorandum 
To 
Dan, Bob S., Ken F., Natalie, Mike 
Dote 
May 5, 1976 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Subject: 
Ted Prim 
PROPOSED REGULATION CONCERNING THE MEANING OF 
"PERSON OR COMBINATION OF PERSONS II WITHIN THE MEAi'1ING 
OF §82013. 
This proposed regulation was motivated by 
Opinion Response 75-205, LumsdoM, in which the Commis-
sion was asked whether a person, 9f the corporation in which 
that person is the majority shareholder, and the business of 
which he is president are a person or combination of persons 
which constitute a committee within the meaning of §82013. 
I believe strongly that we should find a standard which does 
not require a fact intensive analysis to resolve each ques-
tion in this area. 
We do not have the staff to make such 
investigations and, in addition, such an approach provides 
little in the way of predictability for the public. 
Accord-
ingly, subsection (a) of the proposed regs suggest several 
per se rules which may resolve a number of problems in this 
area. 
However, this reg only represents a first shot so I 
am actively soliciting your suggestions on how to approach 
this problem. 
We are required to notice a reg by May 12 so please 
have your comments to me by Monday, May 10. 
Thank you. 
TP :pvp 


o 
M 
T lA 
5, 1976 
18213. 
Persons or Combination of Persons (82013) 
A. 
A person as used in Government Code §820l3 includes 
(1) 
A corporation and its majority shareholder. 
A 
majority shareholder is defined as any person or 
entity which owns more than a 50% interest in the 
voting stock of the corporation. 
(2) 
A partnership and any partner who owns more than 
a 50% interest in the profits or capital stock of the 
partnership exclusive of any interest held by a limited 
partner. 
B. 
A combination of persons as used in Government Code 
§820l3 is two or more persons or entities who enter into 
an agreement or ·iai·t::ial understanding, tacit or otherwise, 
that they will coordinate their efforts or work together 
to receive contributions or make expenditures for 
the common purpose of influencing or attempting 
to influence the action of the voters for or against 
the nomination or election of one or more c~~didates-
or the qualification or passage of a measure.

sections.question_synthetic: How should the FPPC define 'person or combination of persons' under Government Code §82013 to establish clear per se rules rather than requiring fact-intensive analysis for each case?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC proposes regulatory definitions establishing per se rules: a 'person' includes a corporation and its majority shareholder (>50% voting stock owner) or a partnership and partners owning >50% of profits/capital; a 'combination of persons' means two or more entities with an agreement or understanding to coordinate efforts for common political purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum, not a formal advice letter response to a requester. Includes draft regulatory language (subsections A and B) defining key terms. No traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections, but contains substantive policy proposal. Document appears to be a working draft soliciting internal feedback before May 12 regulation notice deadline.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should the FPPC define 'person or combination of persons' under Government Code §82013 to establish clear per se rules rather than requiring fact-intensive analysis for each case?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC proposes regulatory definitions establishing per se rules: a 'person' includes a corporation and its majority shareholder (>50% voting stock owner) or a partnership and partners owning >50% of profits/capital; a 'combination of persons' means two or more entities with an agreement or understanding to coordinate efforts for common political purposes.

embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum dated May 5, 1976, from Ted Prim proposing draft regulations to define 'person or combination of persons' under §82013, motivated by Opinion Response 75-205 (Lumsden), with suggested per se rules to avoid fact-intensive case-by-case analysis and requesting staff comments by May 10.
